body:
This line is the problem: some closures (and likely generators) *can* end up with a `Scalar` layout, and they can even have niches, which then means validity will call `read_scalar` and cause an ICE when we do not treat it as a scalar.

To fix this, we need a way to iterate over the types of all the fields of (all the variants of) a closure/generator. Any suggestion for how to do that? :D I browsed the code for a bit and found nothing.

body:
I don't have strong objections to replacing this `assert` by a `throw` -- it doesn't make the code harder to read or maintain. This should not give rise to the expectation that all ICEs due to misused intrinsics are bugs though, I don't want to see the codebase littered with such checks.

throw_ub seems wrong though; an "invalid program" error seems more accurate. We don't have throw_invalid_format yet, but that should not be too hard to add.
author: RalfJung

body:
> I think if we change anything, we should change this assert into an `if` and a `throw_ub_format`. I'm not sure we should change anything though. @workingjubilee is it ever expected that we'll allow calling SIMD intrinsics from stable code?

No, it is never intended that users will touch these, and we are at least somewhat considering if we can successfully deprecate `#[repr(simd)]` as a concept entirely (as it is also unstable) and just have it be the suite of stable `core::simd` types.
author: workingjubilee

body:
The era of them being "platform intrinsics" came from when there was an intention that `core::simd` and `core::arch` would be a lot closer in design and integration, and that it might be possible to write something like `portable-simd` as a user crate using them. That was 7 years ago, and since then `core::arch` has diverged towards simply recapitulating the existing intrinsics instead, and using more LLVM-particular bits and pieces.

Even if we ever do stabilize these functions, they would be, at best, `unsafe` functions for advanced users, "please only touch these if you would really like to blow your entire foot open".
author: workingjubilee

body:
I wonder if long-term we could implement this on the `rustc` side by getting the "mangling" flavor out of the `datalayout` string.

(Also, I wish both high-level mangling and this lower-level concept weren't both called "mangling" ugh)
author: eddyb

body: Rustc already parses the datalayout string for type layouts.
author: bjorn3

body: LGTM on the typeck/THIR side of things, nothing surprising there.
author: eddyb

body:
Yeah, I was referring to the `m:<mangling>` field specifically. Though, looking at [the docs](https://llvm.org/docs/LangRef.html#langref-datalayout):
> * `x`: Windows x86 COFF mangling: Private symbols get the usual prefix. Regular C symbols get a `_` prefix. Functions with `__stdcall`, `__fastcall`, and `__vectorcall` have custom mangling that appends `@N` where `N` is the number of bytes used to pass parameters. C++ symbols starting with `?` are not mangled in any way.

Yikes, that's a pretty hefty ask. Even if we generated it ourselves we'd probably still want to assert that we got it right by asking the backend what *it* would generate based on its own knowledge.
author: eddyb

body:
> I wonder if long-term we could implement this on the `rustc` side by getting the "mangling" flavor out of the `datalayout` string.

LLVM seems to be moving in the direction of moving the responsibility for low-level mangling to the front-end: https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/54090

body:
As Mara wrote, joining spans with different hygiene to produce diagnostics whose location covers both spans is an important use case of `join` in proc macro error reporting.

Dealing with name resolution in situations that involve different hygiene coming together is not a new problem with `join`. For example a method call like `$a::$b::$c()` already needs to account for there being possibly different traits in scope in each of `$a`'s, `$b`'s, and `$c`'s scope.

If someone needs to be sure that their joined token has some specific name resolution behavior, they should use `Span::join(first, second).resolved_at(wherever)`.

body:
I would like to see some additional C types here, specifically all the ones that are defined in `stddef.h` and `stdint.h`. These two headers are part of the freestanding subset of C which has no OS dependencies.

Specifically I would like to see the [C99 fixed-width integer types](https://en.cppreference.com/w/c/types/integer) and:
- `wchar_t`
- `char8_t`, `char16_t`, `char32_t`
- `max_align_t`

The idea is that anyone interface with C code should be able to blindly use the equivalent `c_*` type without having to think too much about what the exact equivalent Rust type is.

body:
Turns out wrapping_div/rem actually does distinguish between div-by-0 and overflow, but LLVM does not (both are UB). For a second I thought `wrapping_div` relies on the old behavior of the interpreter, but turns out that function actually explicitly checks for this case and avoids even calling the MIR operator. So we are good.

But this is subtle enough that it warrants a specific test.

author: m-ou-se

body:
Hmm, that was unexpected; it looks like the edition-2021 magic fails when the capture is used "by value" but with fully inferred types (the `*&& -> &` place flattening does not seem to occur if the `*&&` place starts off inferred). This includes a move in return position.

Thus, using `move || s` or `|| -> &_ { s }` are both examples that do pass when `T` is only restricted to be `: 'scope`.

  - [Playground](https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=40b548412eb66079b07c009027ef9be7)
author: danielhenrymantilla

body: Is it a problem if `s.spawn(move || s)` works? I can't really think of a good use case, but I'd prefer to not unnecessarily add more restrictions to the return type than necessary. I also think the signature is easier to follow if every lifetime is `'scope` without having to wonder why `F` is `'scope` but `T` is `'env`.
author: m-ou-se

body:
🤔 It all boils down to what does the "scoped runtime" do with the returned value when not joined / and potentially when leaked —I suspect the latter is the only actual area of problems, and the current implementation leaks stuff in that case, so at first glance it looks like it's gonna be fine.

  - If `s` can be returned, then `scopeguard::guard(s, |s| s.spawn(…))` could also be the value of type `T`, and then it _could_ be problematic to have such drop logic run within the runtime's auto-join cleanup _etc._. Basically by using `'scope` we are giving more flexibility to the caller, who oughtn't realistically need it, and which could impair a bit future changes to the implementation so as not to cause unsoundness within that contrived use case.

  - So, _conservatively_, using `'env` is the more cautious and future-proof approach; we could always loosen it afterwards. But I agree I haven't delved too much into "all potential cleanup implementations", and that it is thus also possible that there be no problem whatsoever with that extra flexibility.

To recap:

  - there _could be_ future-proofing, soundness-wise, issues with the more lenient `: 'scope` bound (but it may also be fine);
  - the signature _is_ a bit more complex with `F : 'scope` on one end, and `T : 'env` on the other.

So, one thing that can be done is sticking to `T : 'scope` for now, but adding a mention to this to the tracking issue so that we remember to think about it before stabilization 🙂
author: danielhenrymantilla
m-ou-se

body:
Good catch! :slightly_smiling_face:

___

So, I've been thinking about this, to fully spell out what my hunch / feeling of uneasyness was.

Let's start with the following new names, for the remainder of this post:

 - `: 'env` is `: CanReferToSuffOutsideTheScopeButNotTheSpawner`:
     - renamed `'cannot_capture_inside_scope` in what follows
 - `'scope` is `CanAlsoSpawnScopedThreads`
     - renamed `'cannot_capture_inside_scope_except_spawner` in what follows (together with `Scope` becoming `ScopedSpawner`).

So from a start point of `F` and `T` both being `CanReferToSuffOutsideTheScopeButNotTheSpawner`, we loosen it down to `CanAlsoSpawnScopedThreads` capability to `F`, since we are removing the raw `spawner` handle it used to receive as a parameter.

```diff
- F : 'cannot_capture_inside_scope + FnOnce(ScopedSpawner...) -> T,
+ F : 'cannot_capture_inside_scope_except_spawner + FnOnce() -> T, // equivalent API
```

  - (This change is actually a very good summary of this whole PR, btw :smile:)

Now, you are also giving the `CanAlsoSpawnScopedThreads` capability to the return value of the closure.

```diff
- T : 'cannot_capture_inside_scope,
+ T : 'cannot_capture_inside_scope_except_spawner, // new capability
```

  - If such value is obtained through an explicit `.join()`, then that capability becomes pointless since the `spawner` handle itself is also available.

  - Else, the return value is no longer accessible to the user, and thus its sole API being called, if any, will be its `drop` glue.
      - If the value is leaked, then such capability becomes pointless as well, since not called.
      - Else, the value is dropped around the join-point (but before it, once your PR fixing the soundness bug is merged).

So what granting it the `CanAlsoSpawnScopedThreads` capability achieves is allowing this implicit drop, right before the join point cleanup, to spawn extra scoped threads, leading to thread count changes etc.

  - This is an extra feature of the API, so it can be beneficial if we deem it to be a useful feature to have;
  - It's also a maintenance burden on the scoped threads API, since any time the join logic is touched, implementors will need to be mindful of that rough edge:

    ```rs
    impl<'spawner, T> Drop for Packet<'spawner, T> {
        fn drop(&mut self) {
            // ...
            {
                // Drop the closure's return value: THIS MAY SPAWN EXTRA THREADS
                *self.result.get_mut() = None;
            // ...
            self.spawner.decrement_num_running_threads(...)
    ```

With the caveat that mistakes here endanger soundness, and that removing this `CanAlsoSpawnScopedThreads` capability would be a breaking change.

___

  - So, it's not, granted, _that much_ of extra cognitive burden when dealing with the implementation, but it is _some_.
  - However, the obtained flexibility does feel incredibly niche, imho (moreover, even if I were wrong and that capability were useful, then we could always add it later on: it wouldn't be a breaking change).
  - Remains the argument of "symmetry in the lifetime bounds, for a simpler function signature", which to me is outweighed by the  "caution, extra threads may be spawned during drop glue" subtlety for implementors.
author: danielhenrymantilla

body:
> ```
> // Drop the closure's return value: THIS MAY SPAWN EXTRA THREADS
> ```

I wonder if there's any reasonable implementation where this requires special attention. The point I was trying to make towards the end of my last comment is that implementations need to take care of handling Drop implementations that borrow from `'env` anyway, which means the drop needs to happen before any kind of 'joining' happens, which also covers the case where someone spawns more threads from the Drop impl.

I don't think there is really any difference between spawning threads near the end of `F`, versus doing things in `T`s `Drop` impl, from the perspective of what needs to be taken into account for soundness/correctness. In both cases it's just a thread spawning more threads before it gets joined.
author: m-ou-se

body:
> The point I was trying to make

Yeah I guess I didn't get it 100% the first time 😅; I remained convinced it would lead to a more subtle approach, but as you've patiently mentioned, the subtlety is already there even with `'env`, as in "the true `F` is actually `F + drop(T)`".

Thanks for having put up with my initial skepticism! 🙏 

<sub><sup>Silly GH won't let me mark my own remark as resolved 🤦</sup></sub>
author: danielhenrymantilla

body:
> "the true `F` is actually `F + drop(T)`"

Yeah, that's a great way of putting it.

Part of me wants to say that we should also add some kind of user-visible note about 'scope/'env -- it seems like this is an API that we would *like* new users to Rust to go "cool!" about, and I think the current signature will make them afraid that *all* work in Rust with threads requires complicated lifetime signatures that look pretty opaque -- even to experienced Rustaceans, I suspect :) I'm not sure if putting the note on this type and then linking to it from the other APIs which use Scope is right -- that would be my first instinct, but the more complicated signature is on those APIs (with `for<'scope>`... and all).

author: scottmcm

body:
This is the part from the RFC I was working from:

> it is reasonably common that type inferencing fails to infer the full type of the target type, in which case an explicit type annotation or turbofishing is needed

It's probably better just to refer to the syntax. Type annotations are good syntax, but I think people choose turbofishing for the sake of consistency since most variable declarations don't need a type annotation.

```
If you want to create a collection from the contents of an iterator, the
[`Iterator::collect()`] method is preferred. However, when you need to
specify the container type, [`FromIterator::from_iter()`] can be more 
readable than using a turbofish (e.g. `::<Vec<_>>()`). See the
[`Iterator::collect()`] documentation for more examples of its use.
```

The point of `try_get_cached` was to be inlinable. The rationale was: checking the cache is the fast path, and the slow path can be pushed to the indirect call.
Now that all query return types are `Copy`, we can drop the closure as last parameter as you did. This may decrease IR count a bit.

body:
The library API team already previously discussed and expressed approval of this exact API in https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/82413#pullrequestreview-706596271, so this is good to land as an unstable API. For resolving doubts about tests (https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/94667#issuecomment-1193419310), and to review and iterate on the implementation:

r? rust-lang/libs

In response to the questions listed at the bottom of https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/94667#issue-1160563140: 

> Is there any more iterator-related traits should be specialized?

I wouldn't stress about this at this point. Trait impls can be added separately later if anyone notices one is missing. Please focus on the fundamentals (`next`...) being correct.

The tracking issue (#87155) would be the right place to make a note of what trait impls are outstanding.

> Is the double-space buffer worth?

Yes, this was our preference in https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/82413#pullrequestreview-706596271. I'm sure we'll revisit again during stabilization.

> Should there be `rmap_windows` or something else?

I am skeptical that someone would be able to justify adding this.

> Taking GAT for consideration, should the mapper function be `FnMut(&[I::Item; N]) -> R` or something like `FnMut(ArrayView<'_, I::Item, N>) -> R`?

I'd definitely lean toward the array, but I expect we can hash this out during the period this is available on nightly. Copied to #87155.
author: dtolnay

body:
I think we should find a different name for this field, because whether the buffer is initialized or not can be tracked directly by the `Option`. Seeing a separate field for that makes me confused and it's not clear what exactly we're tracking here.

In practice I think the only reason we actually need a separate field is to track when the underlying iterator is *done*, so that we can avoid calling next on it repeatedly if there's Nones already interspersed. It seems like we could call this field `is_done` or store the underlying iterator in an Option as well? (I think wrapping the underlying iterator in Fuse could also work, but is trickier to implement since there's no is_done method on fused).

Regardless, we should update the comment on this field to reflect whatever we come up with.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
Something feels a little off here - I think we would expect that we successfully initialize and (eventually) return a Buffer if it's elements would be [x, x, x, to_be_returned] where x is an element currently advance_by'd before we start accumulating into the buffer. But I think this code would drop those x elements in the self.iter.advance_by, and so we would not match behavior with calling next() n times.

But I'm not quite sure, this feels tricky - I think we want a test at minimum that this behaves as expected, maybe you can point me to an existing one or add one.

Taking a step back, I think the thing that's making me feel weird is that we're only subtracting 1 from `n` to compute steps, not `N` or `N-1`, which would allow us to fill the buffer with the tail, as seems necessary for yielding the `n + 1`th element.

If the underlying iterator is infinitely long, then map_windows will return -(N-1) elements, right? But that is not an accurate count, just like Skip cannot be accurate in that case.

author: frank-king

body:
> But I think this code would drop those x elements in the self.iter.advance_by, and so we would not match behavior with calling next() n times.

I'm a little confused. Do you mean that whether those `x` elements (in `[x, x, x, to_be_returned]`) are yielded by `self.iter.next()` for `n - 1` times or by `self.iter.advance_by(n - 1)` differs because the former yields and drops those elements but the latter just skips them in place?
author: frank-king

body:
> But I'm not quite sure, this feels tricky - I think we want a test at minimum that this behaves as expected, maybe you can point me to an existing one or add one.

There is a test of `advance_by`: [link](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/94667/files#diff-82642196d9d615b4dcb26384c7d7d8f4a66746358409a21ad27c9926a034f9bdR312).

It tests the `advance_by` called at the very beginning before any `.next()` calls, and also after some `.next()` calls.
author: frank-king

body:
> Taking a step back, I think the thing that's making me feel weird is that we're only subtracting 1 from `n` to compute steps, not `N` or `N-1`, which would allow us to fill the buffer with the tail, as seems necessary for yielding the `n + 1`th element.

Subtracting 1 from `n` is because what we would made for the next window is `n..n + N`th elements (counted from zero), and `Buffer::try_from_iter` should count for one `.next()`, so we need to subtract 1, and then we get the window starting from the `n`th element.
author: frank-king

body: Oh, I think using `Buffer::try_from_iter(self.iter.by_ref().skip(n - 1))` makes more sense here. But it's harder to preserve number of steps remained when failing to collect into the window.

Yes, the `warnings` name seems to behave a bit differently. It looks like it sets the level for all warnings in a given scope. I only could find this documentation: 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17087237/157296495-e4394d3c-b93f-417f-a8d3-9bc67d630698.png)

[source](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustc/lints/groups.html) and [this](https://rust-unofficial.github.io/patterns/anti_patterns/deny-warnings.html). Returning values in kernels doesn't make sense. But right now there are unfortunately no error emitted for attempting to use a return value in a kernel. I've started working on creating a new error which will detect this. 

Am I correct that `bug!` should only be used for compiler bugs? Would it still be beneficial to flag the *user error* by using `bug!`, even before a proper error is implemented? If the answer is yes I will simply add an `is_unit()` and `is_never()` to `rustc_target::abi::TyAbiInterface` and check for those in the place you are referring. I can alternatively add this check in my following PR where the proper error is added as well?

Let me know what you prefer. I'm not familiar enough with rustc to know the right decision.

---

Perhaps unrelated but anyway. My assumption is that the error condition should be detected in `rustc_ast_passes`, do you agree that this is the correct place to implement simple checks on return values?
author: kjetilkjeka

body:
`bug!` is indeed for cases where there's a bug. Your observation that we should be outputting a proper diagnostic here is correct, but ABI code is not the right place for code to do so – typeck would probably be the most appropriate. I'm sure we already have special cases for other ABIs somewhere in there, `rust-call` arguments needing to be a tuple being one IIRC.

Even so, it is a bug if the compiler managed to observe any sort of return value in ABI computation code and so adding a `bug!` is appropriate. Adding a `bug!` now will make the case where there's a `ptx-kernel` function with a return value into an ICE for the time being, but a crash is still better than the compiler doing the wrong thing entirely.

EDIT To add, I don't believe this particular PR is the right place to add the code with the proper diagnostics, I feel like it should be a separate PR, but the `bug!` is definitely something that should be added now.

body:
We blanket enable llvm-static-stdcpp in our CI (see https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/03918badd33d255de806b4a9a8aa75b031ed0738/src/ci/run.sh#L72), so I'm wondering if it makes sense to remove the llvm-tools enablement here -- it's not obvious to me that it *should* be present.

It looks like that was originally added in https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/50336, but it seems like an odd thing to force opt-in users of the build system to with an orthogonal flag. Maybe you have a better sense here though!

I think this somewhat makes this PR a clearer "yes" to me, since if we're migrating to a simpler story of only enabling static stdcpp if asked explicitly, then doing that for everything makes sense. But if that's gated on llvm tools being enabled, it feels weird for that to apply to "not tools".
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
It's a tough call. When you need `llvm-tools`, you'll very often want them with static libstdc++, because otherwise they may just not run on whatever host you try running them on, and you won't be able to tell until a user with an old host is bitten by it. But at the same time, as you point out, there's no _fundamental_ reason they need to be coupled (at least as far as I can tell), which suggests the two flags should be decoupled. I think of this as an anti-footgun of having `llvm-tools` imply `static-libstdc++` because builders may not have thought through the implications of _not_ linking it statically (they may not even be aware of the `static-stdcpp` flag).

Which is all to say — I think decoupling them gives more flexibility to builders, but at the cost of making it harder to get to a functioning build for someone who's trying to replicate the upstream build process. Which of those is more important, I'm not sure.
author: jonhoo

body: Hm. Yes, that makes sense. I think we should probably be changing the default for static stdcpp, then, rather than specifically checking tools here, but that seems like a change that needs it's own PR.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: Oh, yeah, I like that idea! Can submit a PR for that tomorrow. And then you'd want this PR to _just_ switch on `llvm_static_stdcpp`?
author: jonhoo

body: Actually, I think we should do a separate PR that changes the `if` condition to just be `llvm_static_stdcpp` _and_ flips its default. Otherwise, in the interrim (however short) we'll be changing the behavior of the code.

body:
Looks like `Parser` has a `Drop` implementation that emits errors based on the contents of the [`unclosed_delims`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/nightly-rustc/rustc_parse/parser/struct.Parser.html#structfield.unclosed_delims) field and your snapshot is emitting those when dropped? That seems easy enough to confirm.

If that is the case, perhaps make a `diagnostic_snapshot` function on `Parser` which does a `clone` and then clears any state like this (probably just `unclosed_delims`) and use that anywhere that snapshots like these are being made. In fact, perhaps that isn't a great idea because if we want the snapshot parser to become the actual parser (i.e. `*self = snapshot`) then we don't want to lose anything we might want if the snapshot is to be kept - maybe you can experiment with adding a nice API for this that lets us stash away that state that could produce errors when the snapshot gets dropped without throwing away that state entirely, just in case we want to keep the snapshot.
author: davidtwco

author: TaKO8Ki

body: If we do this using `diagnostic_snapshot` then we might be missing `unclosed_delims` that we had before.
author: davidtwco

body:
This would do it but if anyone else notices `diagnotic_snapshot` and wants to use it, they might miss that this is required which could result in a bug later.

You could maybe make a `SnapshotParser` struct (which `diagnostic_snapshot` would give you) containing the cloned `Parser` and the saved `unclosed_delims`. I think a `Deref` implementation to `Parser` would let us use it like we would `self`, you could implement a function on `Parser` (or on `SnapshotParser`, the specifics aren't that important, as long as it is hard to misuse) that takes the `SnapshotParser` and replaces itself with it and restores the `unclosed_delims`. 

Ideally we'd be able to replace all of the other uses of `self.clone()` in the parser for diagnostics with this machinery, and we can put some documentation comments on it that describe how it is to be used, etc.

body: Does this need to be a method on the `Encoder` trait, or can it be a method on individual encoders?
author: bjorn3

body:
It can be a method on individual encoders, and I have made this change.

This did require that `CacheEncoder` be made non-generic, because it needs to call `finish` on the underlying encoder, and if `finish` isn't within the trait then a concrete type is required. This is fine in practice because it always uses a `FileEncoder`.

Other advantages of the change:
- Avoids some `unwrap` calls on `Encoder::finish`.
- Avoids some imports.
- Removes the `Ok` and `Err` associated types from trait `Encoder`.
- Removes need for the `OpaqueEncoder` trait.

body: The size_of_val is a little weird here, but I guess OK -- technically it can just be `size_of::<char>()`. That might be a little more obvious, not sure; it feels like the key point is that `Vec<char>` uses a constant 4 bytes per codepoint (unicode scalar value) whereas UTF-8 uses between 1-4 bytes; that means that it's never worse than `Vec<char>` but sometimes (quite rarely!) equivalent.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: `s[i]` can't actually return a char, since the Index trait [requires `&Self::Output`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/std/ops/trait.Index.html#tymethod.index). `s[i]` is `*s.index(i)` under the hood, but there's no way for String to return a `&char` in that method, since the char doesn't live anywhere in memory.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: I think it might be worth noting here that there are other options (e.g., grapheme indexes), but they are similarly not constant time -- really, the problem here is that because these are not constant time operations, you are unlikely to actually want just the nth grapheme or char -- you probably want to iterate, so we have chars() and there's graphemes() somewhere in the ecosystem.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: I would probably adjust this to more clearly indicate *what* the error is: maybe we can say something like "it is forbidden, and will not compile". This page may be one of the first a newcomer to Rust reads, and I think that extra clarity will be helpful.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body: Yeah I figured `size_of_val` would more semantically convey "the size of the contents" where `size_of::<char>()` ignored the `c` so it didn't seem as obvious to me what I was measuring. But your key point is spot on - if you store each `char` you will probably (but won't always) get worse compression than if you store them in a `String`.
author: mlodato517

body:
That's a really good point I hadn't considered at all! Okay so `s[i]` could return `&char` if `String` was internally `Vec<char>` instead of `Vec<u8>`. We have `Vec<u8>` because of the UTF-8 encoding (because `Vec<char>` would be UTF-32?). So I guess the logic here is:
1. we could've chosen any encoding and we chose UTF-8
2. because we chose UTF-8 we store data as `Vec<u8>`
3. because we store data as `Vec<u8>`, `s.index(i)` can _only_ return a `&u8`

Is that correct? I can't tell if the 2nd option is logically required or if there are other ways of storing UTF-8 data
author: mlodato517

body: Yeah I had intentionally not mentioned graphemes (and I didn't love them in the previous example) because nothing else in `std::` had anything to do with graphemes (to my knowledge!). But I can mention it with the reasoning above!

> you are unlikely to actually want just the nth grapheme or char -- you probably want to iterate

I'm also not 100% sold on this reasoning (That we didn't provide an index operation because you probably wanted to iterate anyway because ... what if the user _doesn't_ want to iterate? Seems like a weird assumption to me :thinking:) but I think

> really, the problem here is that because these are not constant time operations

is incontrovertible. Is it okay if I just focus on that or do we think mentioning the other thing is important? I might just be missing the value! :sweat_smile:

body:
In general 1+2 are correct (there's a 0. which is that UTF-8 is generally a pretty good default due to interoperability with ASCII and common usage throughout e.g. web etc).

We could return anything that's in the vec - for example, &[u8] or &str - but those are weird to return from a particular index accessed.
author: Mark-Simulacrum

body:
I think not mentioning graphemes is probably ok (it might make sense to have some notes on that topic in char docs but probably not mentioning here is good).

The point is that iterators "obviously" expose non-constant time access via nth(...) so you essentially already have that API, and since it's typically not what you want we avoid making an easy to use but actually wrong API by implementing index with &u8 return type.

body:
Can you leave a comment: 
1. saying that this is temporary/hack (or whatever you want to call it), and 
2. explaining what this "means" -- i.e. that this filters predicates referencing the newly replaced placeholder regions

Also, dumb question, what's the difference between `visitor.max_universe() < new_universe` and `visitor.max_universe() <= old_universe`? I guess it probably doesn't change behavior here.
author: compiler-errors

body:
So, the `new_universe` here refers to the universe we *just created* (from the `replace_bound_vars_with_placeholders`). Importantly, we want to *skip* any obligations containing this (so `<=` doesn't work, because `=` means it contains this `new_universe`). Any obligations with a  max universe *greater* than `new_universe` must have been created from a nested HRTB (so maybe from with a `fn`). These are equally weird.

`visitor.max_universe() < new_universe` should be the same as `visitor.max_universe() <= old_universe` though, since `new_universe` *should be* `old_universe+1`, but that's more confusing IMO.

author: petrochenkov

body:
From what I remember from https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/90020 the correct thing to do is more complex, like "link libunwind dynamically if there are dylibs, and otherwise there are options".

Right now we cannot do it properly, that's why the old windows-gnu targets links libgcc through target spec, using `late_link_args_(static,dynamic)`.
Maybe this targets should do the same thing for now, especially given that both specify `crt_static_respected: false` currently.
author: petrochenkov

body:
> > because compiler-rt has to be passed as a path
> 
> Also, could you tell about this in some more detail? Why is this target special in this regard?

With `libgcc` this is rather obvious: path like `/usr/lib/gcc/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu/11.2.0/` is added via `-L` so one can use `-lgcc` and the linker will have no problems looking for it (Clang also searches for GCC libs).

`compiler-rt` however is special here (not in good meaning), it's located in Clang lib dir similarly to `libgcc` so on Linux it's `/usr/lib/clang/13.0.1/lib/linux` but this path is not added via `-L`.  Instead Clang will pass absolute path of the library to the linker (unless using some `-no*` options).
Unfortunately LLVM devs are not in favour of fixing it to behave like `libgcc`: https://reviews.llvm.org/D51440

Alternatively we could call `clang --print-resource-dir` and add it to linker path in every linking process or ship our own compiler-rt build which might be hard to keep compatible with different compiler versions.
author: mati865

body:
Libunwind is special (in good meaning) here, you can link it statically even for 32-bit DLLs and the exceptions will still work across DLLs*.

*unless somebody also links `libgcc_eh`/`libgcc_s`, in that case no matter what you do 32-bit exceptions are broken.

body: Hm, this can change the meaning of the path, no? If you pass a path that starts with `//?/` to `CreateFileW` then it will be parsed similarly to a device path and not be treated as a verbatim path. E.g. passing in `//?/C:/windows/system32/notepad.exe` will work but `\\?\C:/windows/system32/notepad.exe` won't.

body:
This should have been
```
"library/stdarch/Cargo.toml",
```
I think. In Rust 1.61.0, `rust-src` includes `src/rust/library/stdarch/Cargo.toml`, which includes
```toml
[workspace]
members = [
  "crates/stdarch-verify"
```
This didn't show up when testing with `-Zbuild-std` since the [standard list of crates](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/blob/f624095e1c98228a74a165ddb702078c0dd8b81e/src/cargo/core/compiler/standard_lib.rs#L26-L30) to include when building `std` does not include `stdarch`, but it will show up if a user explicitly requests `stdarch`. Or, perhaps more importantly, because of https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/95736, many editors (like IntelliJ) won't treat the root of `rust-src` as a workspace, and will instead recurse into all the sub-crates directly, which then includes `stdarch`.

body: How confident are we in the *positive* test suite for refutable let chains? Should we make sure that the refutable equivalent of every case here is covered?
author: estebank

body: Do we care enough to specify if there are a prefix or suffix? What is the output in a case like `if let Some(x) = Some(Some(1)) && let y = x && let Some(_z) = y {}`?
author: estebank

body:
> Do we care enough to specify if there are a prefix or suffix?

There are different suggestions: either moving the patterns to the parent block, or moving them to the `if`'s body. Second, `leading` vs `trailing` (or prefix vs suffix) is easier to understand for readers than affix I think.

> What is the output in a case like `if let Some(x) = Some(Some(1)) && let y = x && let Some(_z) = y {}`?

Irrefutable patterns that are surrounded by refutable ones are allowed, because there is no low-cost resolution to the lint that you can always suggest. The straightforward resolution is to create nested let chains with the assignments in between, but this is not a low-cost resolution like it is to move the `let` into the body or surrounding body.

Yes, sometimes the inner irrefutable pattern might be something that can be reordered so that it is equivalent to have it in the body or surrounding body, but figuring out the dependencies is a hard problem, especially due to possible side effects in the computation.

Personally I found inner irrefutable patterns useful already despite my limited exposure to the `if_chain` macro.

body: Would `null_mut` itself not be implemented with a cast under strict provenance?
author: SimonSapin

body:
null_mut defers to invalid_mut which in turn is just a cast yeah. The ideal is for everything to funnel into methods with clearer intent/semantics so that automated tooling better understands what you're trying to do and can help catch bugs (i.e. maybe invalid_mut becomes an intrinsic so the compiler can mark some metadata for other tools?)

At very least there are a few lints in the pipeline for people to opt into flagging all the places that do these bare casts as "suspicious" and worth investigating.

Also more stuff using the "right" APIs for what they want to do makes it easier for cursed platforms to do hacks. Like if someone *really* wanted to force Rust onto a null!=0 platform, everything going through null/null_mut/is_null makes that a lot more plausible of an officially-unsupported-but-functional-hack.

author: koehlma

body:
Thanks for pointing that out.

> Also, the definition privacy for these purposes is ambiguous: does it mean private to the module you're looking at or private to the whole crate?

From the perspective of API design, "private" means "private from a view outside the crate." In other words, it includes everything except what's marked with plain `pub`; it excludes `pub(crate)`, `pub(self)`, etc. Since rustdoc is about APIs, I think it makes sense to use that definition of private. @camelid what do you think?

> From the perspective of API design, "private" means "private from a view outside the crate." In other words, it includes everything except what's marked with plain pub; it excludes pub(crate), pub(self), etc. Since rustdoc is about APIs, I think it makes sense to use that definition of private. @camelid what do you think?

author: camelid

body:
Yes, this seems difficult to implement, at least for me. I do not know how to extract from the compiler whether `A` is somehow publicly accessible or not. Does the compiler have this information at all? I also think it makes sense to think about a `pub` item in a private module about being public (after all it is explicitly declared public) but *with respect to* an internal API available to other modules. It is that *internal API* that is documented. While I agree that Rustdoc is about APIs, I like to challenge that it is primarily about the APIs of crates. Documenting private items and crate internals can be highly valuable, e.g., for bringing contributors up-to-speed or to document the internal architecture and design decision behind it. This is my/our use case for this change.

Anyway, what does this discussion entail about the “Restricted Visibility” label? Should that be kept as it currently is or should I change it to “Private”? All items marked that will be marked with 🔒 by the current change are private in the sense that they cannot be accessed from outside the crate.

I think the restricted visibility docs should behave the same way. If you look at the docs you can see that the containing module is private. That the item itself isn't is an indication that it may be public through a re-export. Showing it as restricted may cause people to think it isn't accessible in any way at all.

body:
I've long wanted to look into adding a lint if certain predicates are used, mostly to deprecate the `Eq` and `PartialEq` impls for function pointers.

While the approach of linting when not in canonical queries works right now, my goal - and of @rust-lang/wg-traits afaik -  is to slowly work towards always using queries for trait solving. At this point your code would break.

My expected solution here is to add "this should lint" to the output of a successful selection then let the caller emit the error. I didn't yet look into what's the best way to do this, but without too much thought it might make sense to just change the output of https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_trait_selection/traits/struct.SelectionContext.html#method.confirm_candidate to have a `lint_on_use: Option<SelectionLint>` field. We would then have to propagate this upwards to outside of the query.

Probably makes sense for myself to look into this more, now that someone else also wants that. I will only get to that at some point after the 25th April though.

author: slanterns

body:
`futex_wait` can cause shorter-than-expected timeouts if a signal occurs. A more robust approach would be to calculate the timeout as an absolute `Instant` and then use `FUTEX_WAIT_BITSET` which takes an absolute timeout. The wait itself should be looped so that it is restarted if it returns `EINTR`.

From the futex manpage:
> Note: for FUTEX_WAIT, timeout is interpreted as a relative value.  This differs from other futex operations, where timeout is interpreted as an absolute value.  To obtain the equivalent of FUTEX_WAIT with an absolute timeout, employ FUTEX_WAIT_BITSET with val3 specified as FUTEX_BITSET_MATCH_ANY.
author: Amanieu

body: Does that matter much? According to the [signal (7)](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/signal.7.html) man page, FUTEX_WAIT is automatically restarted with SA_RESTART.
author: m-ou-se

body:
There are 2 issues with that:
- The futex syscall will be restarted with the remaining time at the point where it was interrupted by a signal. This will be inaccurate if the signal handler takes a significant amount of time.
- The signal handler may not have been registered with `SA_RESTART`, in which case we would return too early.

body:
So, my take is this:

* We defined (in this PR) that `T: !Foo` means "there is a negative impl for `Foo`". But I think the *right* meaning is more "`T` can never implement `Foo`" -- that is the question coherence is interested in.
* Right now, coherence is using the more narrow question to answer the bigger one. Since it's the only person asking the question, that's fine, but if we had other bits of code also trying to prove negative predicates, I would prefer if we redefined what negative predicates mean.

body:
```suggestion
    type Error;
    fn alloc_error(layout: Layout) -> Self::Error;
```
I have a feeling this is all you need! One wraps the layout, the other calls `handle_alloc_error`.

Then you can make private methods on the containers (rather than this trait) than take the `Operations` policy parameter, and do the right thing. Those private methods only call other such private methods, to ensure the policy is consistent. And then public methods wrap to hide the policy variable.

I think wit this strategy you can deduplicate *far* more code.

body:
I'm afraid that is the wrong kind of "uninit". `LocalValue::Uninitialized` is a hack that IMO shouldn't exist but is currently required to support unsized local variables. Sorry for the confusion, I will rename this to something more accurate.

This is wrong because it leaks memory (if the local was live), and because it will mean that the local might actually *change its address*, which doesn't make a lot of sense.

Instead this should create a suitable uninit `Immediate` and then set the local to `LocalValue::Live(Operand::Immediate(uninit))`. It will have to be an `Immediate::ScalarPair` or `Immediate::Scalar` depending on the `layout.abi` of the place.
author: RalfJung

body:
Why not have a "full wipe" `dest = Uninit` before starting to initialize the fields?

Also, not sure what purpose automatic initialization serves - is the goal to remove `SetDiscriminant`?

`SetDiscriminant` is still needed to "bless" the ADT value (think partial initialization vs full value initialization wrt borrowck and whether the destructor is active). I don't feel like #94590 provides sufficient motivation - it seems to *assume* `SetDiscriminant` is wrong (it's not) and ignore its current (intended) semantics.

Perhaps the name was poorly chosen, maybe it should've been `FinalizeVariant`?

> 
> Perhaps the name was poorly chosen, maybe it should've been `FinalizeVariant`?

So, the borrowck and thing about destructors is a complete non-issue imo because `SetDiscriminant` does not exist in that part of MIR. It is added during deaggregation and generator lowering, which runs after borrowck and drop elaboration.

This is getting a bit out of scope, but I'd like to argue that generally, MIR falls into two categories:
 1. "Early" MIR - this is the MIR roughly up to drop elaboration/deaggregation. In this part of MIR, we are usually attempting to prove the correctness of some parts of the program - borrowck and drop elaboration both do this kind of analysis. In such cases, it is important to be able to answer the question "is a place fully initialized," because if the answer to this is "no" then we might have to return an error.
 2.  "Late" MIR - this is MIR that begins after deaggregation (or somewhere around there). In late MIR, we are no longer attempting to do static analysis to prove some things about the correctness of the program - instead, we are doing optimizations. This is both typical optimizations, but also generator lowering (much of the complexity of which seems to stem from it trying to be smart). In late MIR, we - as far as I know - never have to prove that a place is initialized. We actually get to assume it, because early MIR has already proved the program "correct" and contains no UB. Instead, optimizations are generally interested in proving other things, like that a place is dead.

The `UninitVariant` rvalue is very clearly tailored towards the second category (and that's good, because that's the only place it appears). It makes proving that a place is dead very easy - it makes proving that a place is harder.
author: JakobDegen
JakobDegen

body:
Maybe the issue is that SetDiscriminant is overloaded with setting the discriminant and marking the value as initialized. We could split this operation into two statements. Then we could have something to deinit the place, something to set the discriminant and something to mark the place as fully initialized (the latter invoking miri's validity checks)

proposal:

1. Reset value by using the new rvalue without a discriminant (so just uninit the destination )
2. SetDiscriminant right where it is right now
3. Assign from the place to itself, and make such assignments special in the sense that they don't do anything beyond guarantee validity (or add a new statement for places that marks them as initialized)


Step 3 can be delayed until we actually move deaggregation earlier, right now it's not necessary

body:
I see @eddyb cc'd me but there's been a lot of comments since then! Is there a need for me to weigh in? 

I would very much like to get more agreement about the formal semantics of MIR; I'm also somewhat of the opinion that we ought to divide out the "analysis MIR" and the "optimization MIR", long term (it may be that the borrow check can even done be done nicely on THIR, actually).
author: nikomatsakis

body:
I don't like conflating the action of setting a discriminant with the "analysis marker" of "all fields have been initialized". Those seem like entirely orthogonal concepts to me. If we need such a marker because otherwise the analysis is too hard to get right, IMO it should be basically a NOP (in Miri it might just check the validity invariant in-place and that's it).

It also seems odd (as before) that generators and enums are treated so differently. But that could be resolved by renaming `UninitVariant` to just `Uninit`, which is strictly more general -- one can simply call `SetDiscriminant` after `x = Uninit`. (I am not sure when `Uninit` would even be generated; my concern here is solely MIR and its semantics.)

@nikomatsakis  The issue is less defining a formal semantics for these operations and more figuring out which are the consumers of the IR and which primitives best fit their needs. And indeed we have some conflict of "analysis" (checking whether variables are initialized) and "optimizations" here.

That said, we do have `Rvalue::Aggregate`, which seems just right to me in terms of an "analysis MIR" -- if only it also supported enums. Deaggregation would then correspond to translating from analysis MIR to optimization MIR, and for the latter we don't need any clearly defined notion of "this is fully initialized" any more.

So, that makes me wonder -- @oli-obk why do you want to move deaggregation into "analysis MIR"? That seems counter-productive to me.
author: RalfJung

author: RalfJung

body:
I just realized there is something fundamentally sketchy about this.

My idea of giving semantics to MIR is that every `Rvalue` (and in general, every expression in value position) evaluates to a "high-level value", which is an `enum` [like this](https://github.com/rust-lang/unsafe-code-guidelines/blob/master/wip/value-domain.md), and then when the value is stored to memory we use the value representation relation to find an encoding of this high-level value in low-level Abstract Machine Bytes. This relation also implicitly encodes the validity invariant.

However, that means that we *cannot have* an "uninit" (r)value of most types, since their representation relation would simply not allow such a value (i.e., would not relate it to any memory representation). This is essentially just as cursed as `mem::uninitialized`...

It *would* still make sense to have a `DeInitPlace`-like operation (`Statement`), since that never constructs a "bad value". But I feel that after `x = ...`, no matter the `...`, `x` should be initialized.
author: RalfJung
JakobDegen
JakobDegen

body:
Ah, yeah, so if this is the model we want then this idea indeed does not work. I have been operating under a different model, which is that `Rvalue`s and `Operand`s are always just a bag of bytes. Operations like dereferencing, `Rvalue::BinaryOp`, and such would then treat the bytes in some interesting way.

I can indeed turn this into a `StatementKind` then, if that fits better with existing models.
author: JakobDegen

body:
Yes, I see that. But I feel like `SetDiscriminant` is a reasonable primitive useful in both situations.

Basically, the hallmark of an expressive and compositional language is that you can put together the building blocks in different ways to get different results. In contrast, having a separate primitive for each new thing is the opposite of compositional. That's why `SetDiscriminant` feels to me like better language design than separate handling of enum and generator discriminants.
author: RalfJung

body:
In general, we certainly want assignment and argument parsing to treat values in the high-level way I described, so it's not just primitive operations. `let b: bool = unsafe { ptr.read() };` is explicitly intended to be insta-UB if the byte read is not 0 or 1.

So this would have to be a special case inside `StatementKind::Assign`, which seems odd.
author: RalfJung

body: Well, we could still make that insta UB at the place to value conversion. The value that is produced does not have to be some high level type in order for `bool` to specify that place to value conversions at that type are UB if they do not yield `[0]` or `[1]`

It's true that generators support that, but it's also true that it'd be *nice* if enums could. (Though it'd be interesting to talk through what would be required -- e.g., would a tree of enum types work?)

In any case, I tend to think of generators as being modeled with a "union", not so much an enum. I'm wondering if that might make sense at the MIR level too?

Anyway, every @RalfJung said made sense to me, it feels like there is a need to step back a bit and do a more holistic write-up here of what is going on and what we want to support. (I'm wondering about things like a compiler team "virtual off site", similar to the off-site meetings we used to have...)

body:
That seems conceptually rather awkward though -- it would mean `=` actually assigns a "raw sequence of bytes" but for some reason `Operand::{Copy,Move}` round-trip through a high-level value and back. This feels very bolted on. (And there are probably other situations where this extra treatment is needed, like function return values.)

I'd much rather define things such that `=` assigns a high-level value -- the right-hand side computes that value, then it is stored to the place denoted by the left-hand side.

body:
`NoDelim` is not a *lack* of a delimiter, it's an *invisible* delimiter (https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/proc_macro/enum.Delimiter.html#variant.None), it must never be dropped.
`rustc` parser dropping the `None` delimiter in some places is a bug (https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/67062).

Could you at least keep it in `all_tts` and drop them as late as possible?
And add a FIXME linking to https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/67062 in the place where they are dropped.
author: petrochenkov

            TokenTree::MetaVarDecl(span, _, None) => {

Style nit: this is probably the most awfully looking pattern in patterns, especially if some arms have it and some not, and I'm not even sure that it disables the "match ergonomics" in the nested patterns.
author: petrochenkov

body:
FWIW, this is a pretty gross violation of the language model, since delimiters are not token trees.

But this is a pre-existing issue since `rustc` parser treats delimiters as regular tokens, if it's rewritten to not do that then the need to put delimiters into a common slice with regular tokens will disappear as well.

It's not actually important what happens in this branch because we don't take the arm of the outer match that includes `match q` anyway (in the code path we miscompile). It's only important that this branch is trivially the same as the other "don't use q" branch - the optimization will see that they do the same thing, and replace the outer match with
```rust
if discrim(p) == discrim(q)
```
I suppose this should really read "do the same thing as in the other fall through branch"

body:
No, it downloads to a temporary directory, then moves the file to `build/cache`. That's necessary to avoid having a partially downloaded file in cache, since bootstrap doesn't verify checksums before unpacking, only just after downloading, so otherwise it wouldn't be resistant to a crash halfway through the download.
https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/beb480d908f091e7c42c866e13641f26b2ee230b/src/bootstrap/bootstrap.py#L67

You can test this by:
1. checking out the branch: `git fetch origin pull/95170/head && git checkout FETCH_HEAD`
2. clearing your download cache: `rm -r build/cache build/$(rustc --version --verbose | grep host | cut -d' ' -f2)/ci-llvm`. This won't delete your build cache, just libLLVM.so.
3. downloading using the new code: `x.py check` with `[llvm] download-ci-llvm = true` in config.toml.

UPDATE: this has been tested on Windows and Mac, it could still use testing on NixOS though.

author: fmease

body:
The problem is that macros can't expand to outer attributes (not only `include!`, but *any* macros). We can extend the wording to mention that, or (ideally), we would check that we're in macro context and be explicit about it (saying "inner attributes are not permitted in macro context", similar in the label, the note can remain the same).

@petrochenkov, what would be the correct way of checking whether one of these comes from a macro expansion? Just checking their span's macro backtrace isn't empty would do the trick and be 100% accurate, right?
author: estebank

body:
I thought about this error again. I _think_ it shouldn't be emitted (just like I originally wrote in the issue before I struck it through).
My reasoning is: You are allowed to `include!` an empty file (basically the `<eof>`, end of file, token) and by extension, we should not emit extra errors (like this one) when we `include!` the sequence

    <invalid_inner_attr> <invalid_inner_attr> <eof>

Those invalid attributes should just be ignored / treated as an empty string.
We are still emitting the `is not permitted in this context` error, so that's fine.
author: fmease

body: Oh, if that works, that would be great. I like the wording _macro context_.
author: fmease

body: Yeah, I agree. We should completely ignore the attribute. It might cause some knock down errors either way (from code that uses the expected expanded code), but at least it won't be outright wrong.
author: estebank

body:
Ok, I tried using [`Span::from_expansion`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_span/struct.Span.html#method.from_expansion) to detect if the inner attributes are macro-generated. However, the method returned `false`. I think this is because [`expand_include`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_builtin_macros/source_util/fn.expand_include.html) just uses the parser method `parse_item` not identifying itself as a macro. Not sure if that's how it works. Maybe the reason is the way the `include!`d [`SourceFile`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_span/struct.SourceFile.html) is loaded: Maybe it's just included as a regular source file and not marked as a “special” file (because it's not special?).

`attr_sp.from_expansion()` returns `false`:
https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/b6ab1fae73a14db17a59e81c532a2b8f048ac159/compiler/rustc_parse/src/parser/attr.rs#L217

Potentially, `from_expansion` is not what I want. I should probably look into [`Span::ctxt`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_span/struct.Span.html#method.ctxt) and see if there's anything interesting (which I doubt since it has to equal `SyntaxContext::root()` according to `from_expansion`.
author: fmease

body:
> Maybe the reason is the way the include!d [SourceFile](https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_span/struct.SourceFile.html) is loaded: Maybe it's just included as a regular source file and not marked as a “special” file (because it's not special?).

I would consider that would be a bug, but currently harmless, because nothing *cares* about the included code being included, I think.
author: estebank

body:
Can you say more about why you picked `Option<Item>` instead of `Option<&Item>` here?  Especially since that's what `peek` does https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/iter/struct.Peekable.html#method.peek

And it'd make sense to me for things like `Repeat` to return a reference to the state, for example.
author: scottmcm

body:
The big reason is that a majority of iterators are completely impossible to do with that type signature. By-reference and by-mutable-reference iterators over collections would completely break, and that's loads of use cases right there.

At first I did that, then I realised how bad of an idea it would be to do that. Essentially, the use cases that would prefer just storing the next value and then referencing it are covered by peekable, and this covers the ones where that seems suboptimal.
author: clarfonthey

body:
But that leads to some implementations using `clone()`, and `has_next()` just calls `peek()` which then clones. That calling `has_next()` might allocate is going to be surprising and certainly more costly than `ExactSizeIterator`'s `is_empty()`.

Edit: Ah, you mention this already in the PR comment.

author: RalfJung

body: I view this as part of provenance. With Stacked Borrows, provenance is more refined than just an allocation ID; that's what the "tags" are for. (And technically we probably would not even need allocation IDs any more; Stacked Borrows tags could be our only provenance.)
author: RalfJung

body:
Shouldn't this say that NULL is forbidden?

read/write at NULL is still UB, even with a ZST. NULL is the one address that the compiler *knows* is not part of any allocation. (At least that's how I thought about it, and it's what Miri checks.)
author: RalfJung

body: I have almost no experience with rust, so I may be mis-parsing this. However, it seems to me that this does indeed need to be `self` and not `self.addr()` if you want the raw pointer representation. For CHERI `self.addr()` will not give the all the pointer bits, only the 32/64 address bits without the metadata.

author: yaahc

body:
I'm not sure about `core::num::EscapeAscii`. Contrasted with the existing stable `core::slice::EscapeAscii` which escapes an ASCII slice, I don't find it clear to say that this one "escapes an ASCII number". The standard library's use of 'number' refers to arithmetic objects, not atoms of text. For atoms of text it tends to call them 'byte'. For example consider the difference between `str.as_bytes()` vs `str.as_numbers()`.

Mentioning @rust-lang/libs-api in case someone has a preference for this or any of the alternatives listed in https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/77174#issuecomment-1032011173: `core::char`, `core::str`, or one I'll add: `core::u8::EscapeAscii`, or that we should keep a `core::ascii` module, or add `core::byte` or `core::bytes`, or …
author: dtolnay

body:
We discussed this PR in today's T-libs-api meeting and our impression was that we would prefer to keep `core::ascii` for now. `core::num` was not favorable to anyone, and there was a vague sense that `core::byte` or `core::bytes` would be valuable (to avoid overloading `core::u8` to mean both the "number" `u8` and the "byte" `u8`), but not if this iterator type is the only thing in there. We would be open to moving into `core::byte` in the future if other things arise that sensibly belong in that module. `core::str` was discussed but this iterator type has nothing to do with the type `str`, `core::char` was discussed but this has almost nothing to do with the type `char`, and `core::fmt` was discussed but this type has nothing to do with the Formatter API, so overall none of those were seen as sufficiently compelling over keeping `core::ascii`.

I'll close this PR, but we would still be interested in just the **documentation** parts of this PR, so I would be happy to accept a new PR containing just those. In particular treating https://doc.rust-lang.org/1.60.0/core/primitive.u8.html#method.escape_ascii as the place where the behavior is described in detail, and having https://doc.rust-lang.org/1.60.0/core/ascii/fn.escape_default.html link to it, instead of vice versa; and having https://doc.rust-lang.org/1.60.0/core/ascii/struct.EscapeDefault.html indicate that it is constructed by the inherent method, not the function.

Thanks!

body:
What I meant to say is that the actual limit is extremely architecture-dependent (and also depends on the alignment of your pointer), so the Rust documentation does not need to be the source of truth for that.
For example Arm's Morello architecture allows for at least 4K (or possible 8, I'd have to double-check) since they use more bits for bounds compared to CHERI-RISC-V.

If you want to be precise you could use `2^(floor(bounds_bits/2)−3)` as the absolute minimum, but in practice that will be higher, since aligned pointers can go further out-of bounds.

C/C++ only allow one-past-the-end pointers, but CHERI is a bit more lenient there. Maybe something like this?
```suggestion
//!   generous buffer on each side of the pointer bounds -- the exact range depends on
//!   the alignment of the pointer as well as the underlying architecture). Note that
```

body:
Miri isn't a platform (in the sense of `--target`), it's more like a codegen backend. Also I am not sure what '"only" semantic' means.^^

The important thing to get across is: Rust programs behave as if they were running on an Abstract Machine that has a bunch of fancy features real hardware does not have. Those fancy features are exploited by the compiler for its optimizations. One of those fancy features is 'provenance'. So when deciding whether a Rust program has Undefined Behavior, provenance is as real as everything else, and should be treated as such by programmers.

That should probably go somewhere central though, and be referenced from this method.
author: RalfJung

body: It might be worth mentioning that provenance *does* exist normatively, but that its exact shape is yet to be determined. The *reason* Miri has provenance is that it *definitely* exists as a concept in the spec. It's not just something Miri does for the fun of it.
author: RalfJung

body: That's rather vague (arguably C's PNVI proposals at least get very close). I'd just avoid statements like this and instead say that specifying provenance is an ongoing challenge in all low-level languages (insert link to C's efforts).
author: RalfJung

body:
Yes, we probably want to have separately noted "provenance" and "experimental model: strict provenance".

I think Rust is tilted fairly strongly to a "provenance not via integers" model, so we can assume things like "integers don't have provenance, pointers do", and that everything else is about how to mechanize that.

I will try to draft some text on this.
author: workingjubilee

body:
I don't think they always require volatile accesses.

Also it's not really an open question, is it? Int-to-ptr casts are fine if they are interpreted as "this generates a fresh provenance and does not interfere with any other provenance generated anywhere else". I.e., if we think of this as `malloc` and "making a noalias promise", all is fine. It's only when you expect the cast to pick up some *previously existing provenance* (i.e. when you round-trip ptr-int-ptr) that we have a problem.
author: RalfJung

body:
> I think Rust is tilted fairly strongly to a "provenance not via integers" model,

I certainly am. :)

However to be fair I am also not sure if I have yet convinced @joshtriplett that Rust definitely has provenance in the sense of an explicit spec ingredient that is 'real' in the Abstract Machine (and alters program behavior!) even when it is not real in the final executable.
author: RalfJung

body: MMIO effectively is never used in a `noalias` fashion in actual practice, it's always `*mut T` not `&mut T`.
author: workingjubilee

body: `0xCAFE as *mut T` is still a "noalias" promise in the sense that it must not alias with any pointer returned by an Abstract Machine allocation operation (local variables, statics, heap allocations).
author: RalfJung

body: When last I saw Josh explain his model, my understanding was that he believed provenance exists and that integers don't carry it (thus 2 + 2 is subject to CSE and reassociation and all that) but that provenance has "out-of-line" accounting that would allow the provenance to "jump" the gap where a pointer lives as an integer, akin to a (possibly more limited/restricted form of) the PNVI-ae* models.

body:
`$ cargo clippy -- -Wclippy::pedantic`
`**warning: babbling commentary ahead**`
` --> library/core/ptr/mod.rs:76:5`
`  |`
`  | //! higher-level than an assembler, but to our knowledge no one has ever been able to square`
`  | ⠀⠀⠀^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^`
Arguably something resembling "provenance" does exist in assembler for CHERI, and also arguably in any situation where violating any other memory protection model could cause program-invalidating behavior based on whether the existing context (either in the pointer or e.g. in the virtual process environment) includes "permission" to access a given memory location. Only in machines specified to have unprotected memory is it strictly permitted to ignore the notion of provenance. Most processes simply respect the implicit rules and the CPU and the OS cooperate to paper over the difference... or they segfault a lot.
`$ █`

body:
(Possibly totally wrong terminology abuse here, have been trying to read more papers and catch up on some of this stuff.)

With apologies to Ralf who I'm sure has a more coherent vision that unifies everything, I am kinda cleaving the memory model into its "spatial" and "temporal" components with this experiment. What I describe here is largely *only* the spatial components of the memory model, leaving all the temporal components as "stacked borrows". (The one exception here is appealing to actual deallocation, which is a temporal property but like the "free square" of memory model bingo cards.)

There are two reasons I find focusing on the spatial component appealing to focus on:

* CHERI as I understand it mostly focuses on taming spatial validity, so getting a solid spatial story is materially useful for physically compiling and running programs to that target.
* Stacked borrows as I understand it mostly focuses on taming temporal validity. Like yes it has the spatial rules embedded in it but the most interesting and hairy problems it tackles seem to be the temporal ones, and it seems like it needs "extra help" from the larger ecosystem to tame the spatial component?
author: Gankra

> (My really maximalist thought is wondering how much raw pointer usage that's just for tagging could be replaced with completely safe wrappers like Tagged<'a, T> that consider how much alignment you get from T and handle masking on access automatically. But this can happen outside the standard library, I hope.)

(Is this considered bad practice to link crates which happen to be yours on rust-lang discussion? Anyway, I have a crate that provides tagged pointer support, [ptr-union](https://lib.rs/crates/ptr-union), though it doesn't automatically detect guaranteed alignment yet, due to stable const generic limitations. I'm currently working on making it work under strict provenance / not use inttoptr.)
author: CAD97

body:
I don't think I quite agree that this can be split in 'spatial' and 'temporal' (Stacked Borrows certainly has aspects of both). However, what you call Strict Provenance is certainly a good warm-up for Stacked Borrows aka "even stricter provenance". Basically, where you describe provenance with exactly one "unique identifier" per allocation, with Stacked Borrows we actually have many identifiers per allocation and some rules that govern which ones are 'active' and allowed to be used when.

So, I would not say they are orthogonal. Rather, Stacked Borrows further "refines" this model of provenance and introduces extra distinctions *within* an allocation. Once we have Stacked Borrows, we don't actually need any other kind of provenance any more.

In both cases however, the core concept is the same: pointers have extra 'invisible' (or not quite so invisible, on CHERI) data attached to them that (a) propagates through all pointer operations and (b) is lost when converting them to an integer (i.e., it is not part of the 'address'). That extra data might simply identify the allocation this pointer "belongs" to, or it might record further information about the "ancestry" of this pointer related to Rust reference types -- the details don't really matter most of the time.
author: RalfJung

body: I 100% agree with your description of Stacked Borrows as a layer on top of "Strict Provenance". So are you comfortable with this text including something to that effect?
author: Gankra

body: By default I prefer to avoid return-type-inference in raw pointer stuff since the cost-benefit is Not Great. Certainly it's fine here, just a habit.
author: Gankra

body:
Specifically something to the effect of:

Provenance is both spatial (pointers have access to specific ranges) and temporal (pointers may become invalidated). This experiment is mostly focused on trying to enforce stricter correctness in the spatial component. It is further refined by *Stacked Borrows* which enforces stricter correctness in the temporal component. Stacked Borrows are necessary to properly described the semantics of references and reborrowing, because borrows are inherently temporal.
author: Gankra

body:
As I said I wouldn't tie this to spatial/temporal. Invalidating provenance on deallocation (e.g. the odd rule for ZST references) is clearly temporal. Stacked Borrows also enforces spatial bounds.

So I would say something more like
> This experiment is mostly focused on trying to enforce stricter correctness on the whole-allocation level, i.e., confining the use of a pointer (and everything derived from it) to the bounds of an allocated object. It is further refined by Stacked Borrows which enforces stricter correctness by making further distinctions *within* an allocation. Stacked Borrows are necessary to properly described the semantics of references and reborrowing, where even pointers to the same allocated object need to be distinguished (and thus carry different provenance).
author: RalfJung

body:
So I think under stacked borrows, where the spatial and temporal components are more tightly coupled, it makes more sense to unify them. However under the weaker strict-provenance model you're doing a lot of very fine-grained spatial reasoning (CHERI literally hangs a slice off every pointer, and I think it's plausible for it to actually do narrowing down to the scale of reborrows) but very coarse-grained temporal reasoning (allocations at best).

When the two are this "out of sync" I think it makes some sense to more explicitly distinguish them. Or at least, I *think* it helps the programmer think about them to understand that like, every pointer "knows" how many bytes it's allowed to access, and that this can be more fine-grained that an allocation. I don't want to get into the actual mechanics of how stacked borrows is introducing new child-ids which can kinda logically be thought of as a new allocation etc etc. Above My Pay Grade And Not In Scope.

body:
Miri without Stacked Borrows basically implements your strict provenance. (Some spatial aspects are missing but it's mostly there.) And the provenance is still exactly a unique ID and that's it. So I wouldn't get hung up on how exactly CHERI implements spatial provenance, I don't think that's how we should describe or think about provenance on the Rust level.

Basically the first bullet describes the observable effect of spatial provenance and the 2nd bullet describes an internal implementation aspect of temporal provenance. That just makes no sense IMO, bullets in a list should all be parallel. I am okay with not going into how provenance is actually implemented, but then the second bullet needs to be reworded I think.

body:
Not 100% sure what you're looking for, so some scattershot alternatives, in decreasing personal preference.

1. Temporal: the allocation that access to these bytes is tied to.
2. Temporal: the allocation that this pointer's validity is tied to.
3. Temporal: the duration of time that access is granted.
4. Temporal: the lifetime of this pointer.
5. Temporal: the borrow this pointer is tied to.

body:
There's certainly situations where you have a fixed address for something mmio/volatile, and other cases where you have a fixed address for "normal memory" that is simply disjoint from any rust memory.
* In the mmio case, using a type or types to help keep track of the volatile access is a very wise plan (which is why I made it a crate).
* In the disjoint-normal-memory case, modeling this as "a weird allocator" that claims a memory region seems sufficient. This would, as usual, be unsafe, and only really work if you're the CPU kernel.
author: Lokathor

body:
The difference is roughly whether just the spacial component makes UAF disallowed or not.

The temporal element is what says that a new allocation/object that *happens* to be at the same physical address can't be accessed, putting the allocation in a temporally disjoint location.

(Under Gankra's model, anyway)
author: CAD97

body:
> any reasoning to the effect of "these are different allocations" is wholy covered by the temporal component.

That's not true I think. You need the spatial component to explain why you cannot merge two adjacent slices for the intersection of their lifetime. "No cross-allocation arithmetic" is a spatial constraint, not a temporal one.
author: RalfJung

body: The explanation "The allocation that access to these bytes is tied to" doesn't even *mention* anything temporal so I don't see how this explains anything re: temporal provenance. The explanation IMO absolutely needs to mention some notion of 'time' or an 'event' that 'invalidates' a previously valid provenance.
author: RalfJung

body:
> vaguely hand-waves the spatial component is bounded to the allocation's actual range axiomatically when the One True Pointer and its fresh provenance is created.

Yes, and the same is true temporally: the One True Pointer has a 'temporal range' (the lifetime of this allocation) and all pointers derived from it inherit that.

This is all very symmetric between spatial and temporal provenance, so I think it should be explained symmetrically. (It might be that CHERI does not implement this symmetrically, but we shouldn't let the implementation details of one particular model of provenance affect this high-level conceptual explanation.)
author: RalfJung
Gankra

body:
> "No-cross-allocation offsets" is spatial but doesn't actually need to know about the allocations in play. All you need to know is that you have a pointer bounded to some slice of memory and offseting or read/writing beyond it is UB.

"No UAF" is temporal but doesn't actually need to know about the allocations in play. All you need to know is that you have a pointer bounded to some range of time and offsetting/reading/writing outside of that range is UB.

In other words, this is wholly symmetric between space and time. (Mumble mumble 2d space-time models of memory something mumble.)
author: RalfJung

body: NB I am still assuming here that forging a pointer from a usize gets you 0 bytes of provenance at best, and that anything more should ideally require some additional annotations. (strict but can be relaxed later)
author: Gankra

body:
Ah ok so my concern here is that it is "weird" to talk about a range of time abstractly, because the *end* of the time period is not known until it happens, in the general case. Which is to say that you only know the end of the time span when the actual deallocation happens. In this sense it feels "right" to describe "time" as intrinsically the existence of the allocation itself.

Would it be more satisfying to you if that was slightly more explicit?

Temporal: The lifetime of the allocation that access to these bytes is tied to.

I know this is overly coarse / not great terminology for handling borrows, but the whole point of strict-provenance as a "subset" of stacked borrows is "thinking about (the temporal aspect of) borrows is really complicated, let's leave that up to stacked borrows".

In this sense stacked borrows is free to refine these definitions to something more precise/fine-grained, right?
