body:
In a bare-metal CHERI world, programs start out with an all-powerful capability (e.g., at hardware reset the default data capability (DDC) and program counter capability (PCC) are initialized to such values). It's up to the language runtime's startup code to subset that into appropriate capabilities for users of C/Rust/etc. For example in CheriBSD, we chop the root capability into a number of sub-capabilities in `cheri_init_capabilities` which is called from `_start` (https://github.com/CTSRD-CHERI/cheribsd/blob/2681a143af26941d54997cfd47e645dcdfbdf3ea/sys/arm64/cheri/cheri_machdep.c#L49). I'd expect a Rust environment targeting bare metal to expose similar capabilities to create pointers for MMIO access and the like. How to expose those capabilities will surely require some exploration.

P.S. The split linked above is less complete that ideal (e.g., the kernel root overlaps with user space), but the split allows us to express our intent and should eventually let us tighten things down further.

body:
It will only need quoting if it contains a `"`, which cannot occur in Windows file names. The problem with `append_arg` is it requires a `OsString` whereas at this point we have the UTF-16 string that was used to test if the file exists. I admit I'm somewhat reluctant to keep switching between encodings.

For now maybe an alternative is to return an error if the script name does in fact contain `"`?
author: ChrisDenton

body:
Can `"` occur in filenames that get interpreted by a driver (e.g. a filesystem driver for a foreign filesystem)?

Could a `\` ever occur at the *end* of a filename that could be executable? Is it theoretically possible to achieve a filename ending in `\` using reparse points, or NTFS Streams, or special `\\` paths? If so, would the `\` escape the `"`?
author: joshtriplett

body: Thank you, this does seem safer.
author: joshtriplett

body:
In [Naming Files, Paths, and Namespaces](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/fileio/naming-a-file) search for "reserved characters". There's a list of characters that are reserved for special use by the OS. These cannot be used in filenames at all. Indeed, `argv[0]` depends upon this fact as a [`"` cannot be escaped in `argv[0]`](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/4ce257ff198d23bdf14e956fbf2fe0fed297201f/library/std/src/sys/windows/args.rs#L79).

A trailing `\` is guarded against [earlier](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/4ce257ff198d23bdf14e956fbf2fe0fed297201f/library/std/src/sys/windows/process.rs#L375) and I cannot think of a way that such a filename could possibly be returned from the OS. Though I guess double checking wouldn't hurt.
author: ChrisDenton

author: petrochenkov

body:
Maybe this enum should have some different name.
It should be short because it's used often.

There are two path resolution modes:
- Speculative mode - we only return the result, but do not report errors or lints, and do not do recovery
- Non-speculative mode - we report errors, do recovery when possible, also report lints, and mark path targets as used (the `record_used` naming comes from this). That's why need some extra data like a node id for linting or a span in this mode.

Maybe call it `enum Finalize`?
It's two letters shorter, not as specific as the old "record used" naming, and we use "finalize" elsewhere in resolve for similar functionality, like `fn finalize_(imports,macro_resolutions)`.

body:
Whoa I had no idea that was allowed. But it doesn't seem equivalent to me. When testing it out, `<() as A>::foo` still requires that `T: Sub` even though the impl has the less strict `T: Super` (you can even leave out the bound entirely on the impl and it still requires that `T: Sub`). [playground](https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=24f05ef3968676a7b6b0b30834bf6c65)

By your analogy, I take it that the non-const default impl is the more restrictive `T: Sub`, and the const specializing impl is the more permissive `T: Super`. But if the analogy holds, then I'd expect us to disallow the use of the const specializing impl in a const context.

Allowing bottom left and allowing top right feel the same to me because both of them are allowing you to do something with some of the impls but not with the others.
author: BGR360

body:
The fact that we ignore the weaker bounds on the impl is something we want to change. This is https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/100706 which currently wants to require an explicit `#[refine]` attribute to use the less strict associated items.

yeah, this table is the behavior i expected:

|| General impl, not const  | General impl, const |
|---| ------------- | ------------- |
| More specific impl, not const | Currently allowed  | should be disallowed  |
| More specific impl, const  | should be allowed  | should be allowed |

You could think of `const` functions as having the `trait NonConst: Const {}` as a bound. A function which is not const has more requirements for the caller (as they are not allowed to use this function in a const context).

The intent is not "doing something with some of the impls but not with the others". The intent is "loosening the restrictions on assoc items in specialized impls is ok". If you have some specialized impl, that impl should be usable in all ways the default impl can be used, and maybe more (by loosening a restriction). You should not loose capabilities by going from a default to a specialized impl.

does that explanation make sense to you? it feels somewhat difficult to explain :grin::sweat_smile:

body: The `io::copy` optimizations rely on specialization, currently we're usually not documenting those since specialization itself isn't stable and so may change in the future. At a minimum there should be a caveat in there that it's only current/not guaranteed behavior.
author: the8472

body:
I see. And even beyond Rust not necessarily committing to this there are a bunch of very complicated conditions around whether `copy_file_range` will actually work or whether it needs a fallback.

I was trying here to follow the direction of <https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/io/index.html#platform-specific-behavior>: 

> Many I/O functions throughout the standard library are documented to indicate what various library or syscalls they are delegated to. This is done to help applications both understand what’s happening under the hood as well as investigate any possibly unclear semantics. Note, however, that this is informative, not a binding contract. The implementation of many of these functions are subject to change over time and may call fewer or more syscalls/library functions.

In particular, I was looking at the documentation and then the source to understand if the stdlib would even _attempt_ to use the handoff syscalls or whether there would be any benefit to trying it myself.

How about if I add to the comments

> The use of these syscalls is not guaranteed and may change in future releases.

?
author: sourcefrog

body:

You're correct that it incorrectly fails here.

author: CAD97

body:
I don't know; it would probably depend greatly on what information is available at the callsites.  (Whether the `self.size` or the `n` is more likely to be constant.)

Hmm, this might be too cute, but what about:
```rust
let n = (self.size << 1).checked_mul(n).ok_or(LayoutError)? >> 1;
```
along with a whole bunch of tests, of course.

(The shift can't overflow because `self.size` always has the high bit zero, so the unsigned overflow stops at the right place, and the shift back down at the end just shifts out a low-bit zero to fix the scale.)

author: CAD97

body:
Godbolt comparison: https://rust.godbolt.org/z/a3sb3v3sK

Observation putting this together: we don't have to check `isize:MAX` here; `from_size_align` will of course now do that for us, meaning the implementation *does not change* from `HEAD`, since it was using the safe `from_size_align` already.

The size check when nothing is known is fairly clearly the worst option of the correct ones (3 jumps vs 1). Strangely enough, it looks like doing the check that way is potentially *better* than the `isize::checked_mul` (1 jump vs 2); based on staring at Godbolt it looks like this is because with the `usize` mul we get an overflow check error return and handle `from_size_align` with `cmov`s, whereas with the `isize` mul we need to insert a check and bail for an input high bit first, then do the same thing.

I think reverting this to HEAD beats out the bit tricks by obviousness of implementation alone, given the minimal output difference, but it also looks to me like the bit twiddling impl ends up executing all the same assembly instructions but with an additional two to shl and shr, such that an overflow occurs and returns sooner. If anything, this nano-optimizes for the overflowing case, which we expect to be the less likely case anyway.

Hmm.

So I don't like the syntactic comparison for equality that is throughout this code -- I rather prefer the older code, and I would rather express the correctness conditions logically. Certainly in a-mir-formality that is what I expect us to do. (Something like: for all instances of the struct, the drop impl conditions are met.)

That said, the code is cleaner this way, and the only case I could come up with where using syntactic checks would be a problem seems to [already be rejected](https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2021&gist=227432640a3a031c45fa2304a6889333). I consider that a bug, but it's pre-existing and more of a problem with the other drop code.

```rust
struct Foo<T>
where
    T: Iterator,
    T::Item: Send,
{
    t: T,
}

impl<T, I> Drop for Foo<T>
where
    T: Iterator<Item = I>,
    I: Send,
{
    fn drop(&mut self) { }
}

fn main() { }
```

**Do we have a test like this one? If not, can we add one? Otherwise, r=me.**

body:
Now, since I've come into contact with cross-crate documentation issues (#95717), I think this code path (`DocumentationNotBuilt`) is reached when someone implements a trait from an external crate whose documentation wasn't built. I don't know if that ever happens with `cargo doc` but it sure happens with plain `rustdoc`:

Let there be crate `a` defining `pub trait Tr { fn f(); }` and crate `b` depending on `a` containing `pub struct Ty; impl a::Tr for Ty { fn f() {} }`.
Now execute

```sh
rustc a.rs --crate-type=lib --crate-name=a --edition=2021
rustdoc b.rs --crate-type=lib --crate-name=b --edition=2021 --extern=a=liba.rlib
```

In such case, the `f` in the documentation of `impl a::Tr for Ty` (in `doc/b/struct.Ty.html`) does **not** have an `href` attribute since the link would otherwise be broken. And this is the code region responsible for that.

body:
I think leaving evaluation order indeterminate is a bad idea. We should specify some order eventually, and probably it should be left-to-right. (That's what Miri does.)

The disjointness thing can be enforced without leaving the order indeterminate, using the aliasing model instead. That's better because it can be checked by Miri.
author: RalfJung

body:
I don't think CFG paths are relevant. What's relevant is the actual sequence of statements that are executed when the function runs. There's basically a boolean flag dead-or-alive for each local that starts out 'dead' for locals with annotations, and that is set appropriately by these annotations. Accessing (evaluating the place expression) of a local is UB if it is currently 'dead'.

Actually it's more than that, each StorageLive allows the local to be put at a different location in memory.

body: That's what Miri does, and I think it is the right model.

Come to think of it, we should probably mention that we often don't want to "remove" UB in MIR optimizations in general, since keeping it visible to the codegen backend is important.

body:
`crate::Type::AssocItem` now takes the set of traits in scope at the point where it's written, and not elsewhere (in the crate root module). This is the change that fixes https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/78696.

So we no longer need to collect traits in scope at arbitrary root modules.

(The old buggy behavior was caused by replacing `module_id` with `CRATE_DEF_ID` too early.)

body:
I am on board with this API. Next steps are:

- I think this is the kind of method that would benefit from a usage example in the rustdoc.
- Needs a tracking issue.

Some considerations to note in the tracking issue for discussion before we could stabilize:

- Just change the not-yet-stable `Drop for DrainFilter` to keep rest?
  - Advantage: usually what you want (??)
    - I don't have a good picture what the use cases involve drain_filter and *not* exhausting the iterator obtained from it.
  - Disadvantage: inconsistent with stable `Drop for Drain`
  - If you want to remove rest (matching the current unstable behavior of drain_filter) then you'd need to write `.foreach(drop)` to explicitly drop all the rest of the range that matches the filter.
- `&mut self` instead of `self`?
  - If you're holding a `Drain` inside a struct and are operating on it from a `&mut self` method of the struct, `keep_rest(self)` is impossible to use. :(
    - You'd want something like `mem::replace(&mut self.drain_filter, Vec::new().drain(..)).keep_rest()` but the borrow checker won't like that.
    - Failing that, you'd need to change your `Drain` field to `Option<Drain>` and use `self.drain_filter.take().unwrap().keep_rest()` along with `unwrap()` everywhere else that the drain is used. Not good.
  - We'd need to define behavior of calling `.next()` *after* `.keep_rest()`. Presumably one of:
    - `.next()` returns `None`
    - this is considered incorrect usage and so `.next()` panicks
    - `.keep_rest()` sets a flag inside the Drain which Drop will use to decide its behavior, and you're free to continue accessing iterator elements in between.

body: FWIW I think the way to avoid making equality complicated is to ensure there is a normal form: *all* (parts of( constants of type `&str` (for example) must be a `SliceOrStr`, they can *never* be a lot of `Leaf`s. That ensures that when comparing valtrees of equal type, we never have ambiguity about whether something should be represented as `Leaf` or `SliceOrStr`.

"dereferencing a place" does not make a lot of sense as a concept, and the code doesn't even call `deref_operand`. So something seems odd here.

Is this supposed to be short for something like
```
self.deref_operand(&self.read_immediate(&src.into())?.into())?
```
? I don't think we need a dedicated function for that, it seems short enough -- and the act of reading the immediate (i.e. the ptr) from the place should rather be left explicit, IMO.

Both `ImmTy` and `MPlaceTy` can be converted to `OpTy` via `into`.
author: RalfJung

body:
Wow that is an odd function. Please add a doc comment explaining it.

The name also doesn't really reflect what it does -- it seems to specifically assume that the pair has a pointer as its first component and a length as its second component? It would probably be less complicated to start by calling `ref_to_mplace`, and then converting the *place* to an allocation-range pair. That also makes a lot more sense in general: every `MPlace` does correspond to a range in some allocation. So I would suggest a signature like
```rust
fn mplace_to_alloc_range(..., &MPlaceTy<'tcx>)
-> (&Allocation, AllocRange)
```

This function has the extra twist that the pointer *must* point to already interned memory. It might not even need `&self` and can work with just a `tcx`?
author: RalfJung

body: The reason why I created this function was to get access to the `AllocRange` for the Allocation. Afaict there isn't a function that gets the `AllocRange` from an `MPlace`, so I had to get the length of the slice through the `ScalarPair`. We actually start out with an `MPlace`, so if I knew how to 'convert the place to an allocation-range pair' that would make this function unnecessary and would greatly simplify the logic.

body: We do need to encode the length of the slice in the valtree, but don't need that for non-slice Refs. This is necessary for the Valtree -> ConstValue/ConstAlloc (whatever we end up with) conversion.
author: b-naber

body:
What about types like `&(i32, [i32])`?

I think you might need something general for the *metadata* of a wide reference, but
- not just slices/str have metadata
- that shouldn't affect the way the pointee is handled recursively
author: RalfJung

body:
Thinking about it some more -- why do you even need to encode the length? It seems redundant. For `&[1,2,3]` at type `&[i32]`, I expect the valtree would be something like
```
   (root)
  /  |  \
1    2   3
```
This contains all the information needed to reconstruct the original value.
author: RalfJung

body: @RalfJung You're right, we can just read off the length of the slice from the length of the branch slice. Thanks for noticing that we don't actually need to encode the length in the valtree. But I still believe that we need to distinguish between refs corresponding to slices and other refs, because of the indexing into the place (we need to use `mplace_index` for slices and `mplace_field` for everything else), though we can do that in `branches` if you prefer that.
author: b-naber
